title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Before the division of Korea in 1945, Gangwon (강원도) and North Korea's Kangwon (also 강원도) formed a single province. It is the least densely populated province in South Korea. The capital of the province is Chuncheon (춘천시).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: The name of this province literally translates to 'The Area Surrounding the Capital'. Gyeonggi Province (경기도), Seoul (서울특별시) and Incheon (인천광역시) are collectively known as Sudogwon (수도권), and make up about half the population of South Korea. The capital of the province is Suwon (수원시).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: South Gyeongsang Province is home to a UNESCO World Heritage Site: Haeinsa (해인사), a Buddhist temple that houses the Tripitaka Koreana. The capital of the province is Changwon (창원시).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: This is the homeland of the former kingdom of Silla (신라), one of the Three Kingdoms of Korea. It is perhaps the hottest part of Korea in the summer due to being surrounded by the Taebaek Mountains (태백산맥) in the east and the Sobaek Mountains (소백산맥) in the west. The capital of the province is Andong (안동시).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Considered the birthplace of Korean democracy, after the events of the Gwangju Uprising in 1980. TalkToMeInKorean's founder and CEO, Hyunwoo Sun (선현우) comes from Gwangju. It is the 6th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Due to its extreme summer temperatures, Daegu is nicknamed Dae-frika (대프리카; Daegu + Africa). Famous folk singer Kim Kwang-Seok (김광석) was from Daegu. Nowadays, you can visit a street commemorating him in the city. It is the 4th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Located in the heart of South Korea, Daejeon is a hub of transportation. There are 18 universities in Daejeon, including KAIST, which is one of the best universities in the country. Young Koreans have nicknamed it '노잼도시', meaning 'boring city', due to a lack of things to do (at least when compared to Seoul). It is the 5th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Known for its amazing beaches and seafood. Home to Asia's biggest film festival (Busan International Film Festival), the world's largest department store and 6th busiest port in the world. It is the 2nd largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: The capital of South Korea. It has a population of ~10 million, housing one-fifth of the population of South Korea. It is one of the top 10 most expensive cities in the world.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Founded in 2007 as a new planned capital of South Korea, to ease congestion in Seoul and encourage investment in Korea's central regions. Hence, several government ministries have been relocated to Sejong. By 2030, the population is expected to be ~500,000 and all government institutions will be moved there. It is the 51st largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: The industrial powerhouse of South Korea. It hosts the world's largest automobile assembly plant, operated by the Hyundai Motor Company, the world's largest shipyard, operated by Hyundai Heavy Industries, and the world's third-largest oil refinery, owned by SK energy. It is also known for its whaling heritage, which you can learn about at the Jangsaengpo whale museum (장생포 고래박물관). It is the 8th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: A major transportation hub in North-East Asia, with Incheon International Airport (인천 공항) and Incheon Port (인천 항구). It has the best Chinatown in South Korea, which is where jjajangmyeon (짜장면) was born. It is the 3rd largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: There are almost 2000 islands along the coastline of South Jeolla Province, three-quarters of which are uninhabited. The largest city in the province is Yeosu (여수시). However, the capital is Muan County (무안군).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Home to the Jeonju International Sori Festival (전주세계소리축제), which is South Korea's main traditional music festival. It has been ranked among the top 25 best international festivals in the world several times. The capital of the province is Jeonju (전주시).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Due to it being isolated as an island, the Jeju people have developed a culture and language distinct from mainland Korea. Jeju dialect (제주어) is not mutually intelligible with other dialects of Korean. The air route between Jeju and Gimpo Airport (domestic airport that serves Seoul) is the busiest in the world. The capital of the province is Jeju City (제주시).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: This is South Korea's most productive province, with the highest GDP per capita. There are a number of old temples, including Gwanchok-sa (관촉사), which is home to the largest stone Buddha in Korea. The largest city in the province is Cheonan (천안시), but the capital is Hongseong County (홍성군).

title: Administrative divisions of South Korea
notes: Very mountainous and is South Korea's only landlocked province. The province specialises in producing ginseng and tobacco. The capital of the province is Cheongju (청주시).

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: It is the 29th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Famous for Osan Market (오산시장), one of the largest in the country, which has been in continuous operation since 1792. In 1950, this is where North Korean and US forces first clashed during the Battle of Osan (오산 전투). It is the 47th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Home to Everland (에버랜드) and Caribbean Bay (캐리비안 베이), South Korea's most popular amusement and water park. The Korean Folk Village (한국민속촌) is also located here. It is the 11th largest city in South Korea, and has recorded the fastest population growth of any city in the country during the 21st century.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: The capital of Gyeonggi Province (경기도), and the only remaining completely walled city in South Korea. Samsung Electronics are headquartered here. It is the 7th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: This was where the Battle of Kapyong (가평전투) took place, a major battle of the Korean War.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Hwaseong has the largest area of farmland of any city or county in Gyeonggi Province. It was the site chosen for the Universal Studios South Korea theme park. However, the project has now been scrapped. It is the 19th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Anyang has South Korea's only "singing road". Grooves cut into the ground cause tactile vibration when driven over, which allows drivers to hear the tune of "Mary Had a Little Lamb". It is the 20th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Not to be confused with Gwangju (광주광역시) in South Jeolla Province (전라남도). It is the 34th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Some islands lie within the jurisdiction of the city, including Daebu Island (대부도), which has been a film location for several famous K-Dramas such as Boys Over Flowers (꽃보다 남자). Kim Yeon-Koung (김연경), the captain of the South Korean women's national volleyball team is from Ansan. It is the 15th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Known as "The City of Masters" for its production of brassware and arts & crafts. Asian grapes, ginseng and rice are produced here. It is the 52nd largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Second most densely populated city in the country after Seoul. The city promotes itself as the cultural centre of the Seoul Metropolitan Area, as it is the home of the Bucheon Philharmonic Orchestra and hosts the Bucheon International Fantastic Film Festival every July. Mamamoo's Moonbyul (문별이) is from Bucheon. It is the 13th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Located north of Seoul, Dongducheon is a strategically important city for the defense of Seoul. Since 1999, it has hosted Dongducheon Rock festival every year, one of the biggest music festivals in the country. It is the 75th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: There are many things to do in Goyang including hiking up one of the mountains that is distinctive of Seoul's skyline, Bukhansan (북한산). Many celebrities call this their hometown, including BTS's leader RM. Not to be confused with the Korean word for "hometown", which is 고향 (gohyang). It is the 10th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: 73.2% of the city is green space, mainly due to Surisan (수리산). Olympic figure skater Yuna Kim (김연아) was born in Bucheon (부천시), but has lived in Gunpo since she was 6 years old. It is the 37th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: The Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty are located in the city, as is Achasan (아차산). Twice's Jihyo (박지효) is from Guri. It is the 53rd largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Many attractions with misleading names, such as Seoul Grand Park (서울대공원), Seoul Land (서울랜드) and the National Museum of Contemporary Art (국립현대미술관) are all located in Gwacheon, not Seoul. BTS's Jin was born in Gwacheon. It is the 83rd largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Mainly residential as it is a commuter town for people who live in Seoul. It is home to one of the world's largest IKEA stores (59,000m2), as well as a Costco and Lotte Premium Outlet. It is the 31st largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: South Korea's second largest shopping mall, Starfield Hanam (스타필드하남), is located here. It is the 59th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Along with Yeoju (여주시), Icheon is known as a center of South Korean ceramic manufacturing and is a UNESCO City of Crafts and Folk Art. This is the place to go for some quality pottery. It is the 49th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Rapidly developing a reputation as a regional centre of excellence for organic farming. In September 2011, the world's first organic agriculture museum was opened here. It is the 18th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: North Korea can be seen from Mount Dora (도라산) in the city. Many attractions are in Paju, including Heyri Art Valley (헤이리 마을) and Gloucester Valley Battle Monument (파주 영국군 설마리전투비). Many foreigners live and work at the "Paju English Village" (파주영어마을), an immersion-style English camp for kids. It is the 28th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: An extremely mountainous city. Located here is Korea National Arboretum (국립수목원), commonly referred to as "Gwangneung Forest" (광릉숲) by the public. It is the 57th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Home to a large concentration of US troops. It is the 23rd largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: This was the first ever planned city in South Korea. It is the 12th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Notable landmarks include Oido Island (오이도), Soraesan (소래산), and the port and inlet of Wolgot (월곶). It is the 25th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: An important choke point across the traditional invasion route from North to South Korea. It is well known for its budae-jjigae (부대찌개), literally "army base stew". If you like the dish, then you can visit "budae-jjigae street". Famous singer Taeyang (태양) is from Uijeongbu. It is the 26th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Rail transportation is important in Uiwang. It is home to the Korea Railroad Museum (철도박물관) and Korea National Railroad College (한국철도대학). It is the 58th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Hosts the Yangju Snow Festival (양주 눈꽃축제) annually. It is the 50th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Gyeonggi Province
notes: Along with Icheon (이천시), Yeoju is known as a center of South Korean ceramic manufacturing and hosts the World Ceramic Exposition every year. It is the 69th largest city in South Korea.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Named after the East gate (동대문) in Seoul's city walls, which was one of the four gates of the capital city in the Joseon dynasty era.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: The symbol of this district is the snowy heron, meant to symbolise the "clean, beautiful and noble spirit" of Dongjak's people. You can find stickers of a cartoon character named 'Roya', a baby snowy heron, on signs, banners and light poles throughout Dongjak district.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: One of the neighbourhoods in this district, Amsa-dong (암사동), hosts a ~6000 year old prehistoric settlement site.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Until the early 1980s, the Greater Gangnam Area was the least developed in Seoul. Nowadays, it is well known as a wealthy and highly influential part of the capital. The price of real estate is 3.5x the national average and accounts for almost 10% of South Korea's total land value.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: One of the neighbourhoods in the district, Gonghang-dong (공항동), literally meaning 'airport neighbourhood', is where you will find Gimpo Airport (김포공항), which is Seoul's secondary airport. Korean Air are also headquartered here.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: The name Guro originates from the legend that nine (Gu/구) old men (Ro/로) enjoyed longevity in the district.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Once a rural area, rapid industrialization and population booms in the 1950s and 60s transformed the district into a densely populated residential and industrial area. Redevelopment projects from the 1970s onwards developed slums into the uptown residential district that it is today. Seoul National University's (SNU; 서울대학교) main campus is located here.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Home of Konkuk University (건국대학교), Sejong University (세종대학교) and Children's Grand Park. The southern part of the district is packed with high-rise apartment buildings typical of the Seoul skyline, yet the central and northern parts are centers for light industry and manufacturing.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Named after a major local street, Jongno, meaning "Bell Street". This district is the cultural and historical heart of Seoul. It contains several palaces as well as the current Korean president's residence. You can also find the well known Cheonggye Stream (청계천) here. It is a popular tourist destination.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: This is the historical city centre of Seoul, with a variety of old and new buildings and features. It is the least populous and smallest district in Seoul. Here you can find Namsan (남산) and Myeongdong (명동), two of the most popular tourist spots in the city.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Home to several universities and government buildings. It is most well known for the Hongdae (홍대) club district surrounding Hongik University and the Seoul World Cup Stadium.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Despite being called no-one (badum-tsh), this district has the highest population density in Seoul (17,000/km2). It hosts several educational institutes, including Seoul National University of Science and Technology, giving it the reputation of being the "educational district" of Northern Seoul.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Generally referred to as part of the Greater Gangnam Area along with Gangnam (강남구) and Songpa District (송파구). The Supreme Court of South Korea, National Library of Korea and Seoul Art Centre are all located here. The infamous 1995 collapse of the Sampoong Department Store also happened in the district. It hosts a small French enclave called Seorae Village (서래마을), which has a large concentration of French residents, European-style restaurants, wineries and cafes.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Named after Donuimun (돈의문), one of the 8 gates of Seoul. There are a total of 46 educational institutes here, including Yonsei University (연세대학교) and Ewha Women's University (이화여자대학교). The leader of K-Rock band Boohwal (부활), Kim Tae-Won (김태원) is from Seodaemun.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: K-R&B singer/songwriter Bumkey (권기범) and Red Velvet member Wendy (손승완) were both born in Seongbuk-dong, Seongbuk District (성북구, 성북동).

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Seoul Forest (서울숲) is located in the district.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Generally referred to as part of the Greater Gangnam Area along with Gangnam (강남구) and Seocho District (서초구). Songpa has the highest population of Seoul's districts (~650,000). This was the center for the 1968 Asian games and the 1988 Olympics, so you can find the Seoul Olympic Stadium (서울올림픽주경기장) and Olympic Park (올림픽공원) here. Lotte Tower, the world's 5th tallest building, is also located in the district.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: The centre of the district is the Mok-dong (목동) area, home to numerous shopping outlets, bars, restaurants, an ice rink and residential buildings occupied by middle-upper class families. Considered one of the better places to live in Seoul. Hyperion Towers, Tower A, located in the district, is one of the tallest purely residential buildings in the world.

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: For several decades, Yeouido-dong (여의도동) in the district has been considered the financial center of Seoul. The origin of the district's name is uncertain, but the first two syllables are thought to be from 'Yeongdeung' (영등/靈登), meaning "divine ascent", and the third syllable is 'Po' (포), representing the bank of a river (浦), referring to the district's position on the Han River (한강).

title: Administrative divisions of Seoul
notes: Home to Itaewon (이태원), an ethnically diverse part of Seoul where you can find various international cuisine. In 2020, there were a total of 21,067 foreign residents living in the district. It is also home to Seoul Central Mosque and Homo Hill, Seoul's Gay Village.

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: Least populated county in South Korea. Consists of the island Ulleungdo (울릉도) and 44 smaller islands in the Sea of Japan, including Dokdo (독도), a group of small islets controlled by Korea, though Japan also claims sovereignty over them.

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: A group of islands in the Yellow Sea that are a county in Incheon Metropolitan City (인천광역시). Four of the islands are very near the Northern Limit Line (de facto maritime boundary between North and South Korea).

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: The name of the province was derived from the principal cities of Chungju (충주) and Cheongju (청주). The provincial capital was Gongju (공주시), which was also the capital of the kingdom of Baekje (백제) from 475-538 CE.

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: The name of the province was derived from the principal cities of Gangneung (강릉) and Wonju (원주), which was the provincial capital. After the Korean War, it was divided into two provinces with the same name; one in the South and one in the North.

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: The birthplace of the kingdom of Silla (신라). Also the birthplace of seven South Korean presidents, including Moon Jae-In (문재인).

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: After being renamed several times, the name of the province was derived from the principal cities of Hamheung (함흥) and Kyongsong (경성).

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: Literally meaning "Yellow Sea Province", the name of the province was derived from the principal cities of Hwangju (황주) and Haeju (해주).

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: The entire mainland region of the province was referred to as Honam (호남), meaning "South of the Lake", which is still commonly used today.

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: The name of the province was derived from the principal cities of Pyeongyang (평양) and Anju (안주). Pyeongyang, now known as Pyongyang, is the capital of North Korea.

title: 8 Historical Provinces of Korea
notes: The province started to rise as the central region of Goryeo (고려) as King Taejo of Goryeo (고려 태조) (the kingdom following Silla) set up the capital there. It is geographically quite similar to the modern day Gyeonggi Province.

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: Some of the most important historical sites in Korea are here. Ganghwa Island (강화도) has many dolmen, which have been designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. ~70% of the citizens in the county are farmers. Their specialty is the Ganghwa turnip (강화순무).

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: The county consists of 111 inhabited islands and 719 uninhabited islands, which account for 25% of all the islands in South Korea. It is known for skate (fish) and cheonilyeom (천일염, a type of sea salt).

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: Along with Jindo Island, the county contains an archipelago of 230 small islands, 45 of which are inhabited. Winner of 2019 reality TV show 'Miss Trot' Song Ga-In (송가인) is from Jindo County.

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: The largest island and county seat, Wando Island is famous for Cheonghaejin (청해진, literally "Blue sea headquarters"), the former headquarters of Jang Bogo (장보고), a 9th century Korean historical figure who dominated the Yellow Sea, and controlled much of the trade between Silla, Heian Japan, and Tang China for decades.

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: There is a unique German Village (독일 마을), which was built for Korean residents who returned from living in Germany in the 1960s. Nearby is the Mulmi coastal road (물미해안도로), one of Korea's most beautiful coastal drives.

title: Islands of South Korea
notes: Geoje Island is connected to the mainland via several bridges and underwater tunnels, and has plenty of tourist attractions. The 14th President of Korea, Kim Young-Sam (김영삼), was from Geoje City.

title: Rivers of South Korea
notes: Flows through both North and South Korea, crossing the Demilitarized Zone (DMZ). It has a length of ~482km, making it the 4th longest river in South Korea.

title: Rivers of South Korea
notes: The longest river in South Korea, with a length of 510km. It passes through the major cities of Daegu and Busan.

title: Rivers of South Korea
notes: Probably the most well known river in South Korea, as it passes through Seoul. It has a length of 494km, making it the 2nd longest river in the country.

title: Rivers of South Korea
notes: 397km long, making it the 3rd longest river in South Korea.

title: Mountains of South Korea
notes: The highest peak in the Sobaek Mountains is Jirisan (지리산), which sits at the southwestern limit of the range. This formed the traditional boundary between Honam (호남) and Yeongnam (영남) regions.

title: Mountains of South Korea
notes: The Taebaek Mountains stretch across both North and South Korea and are the main ridge of the Korean peninsula. The total length of the mountain range is ~500km and it has an average height of ~1000m.

title: Mountains of South Korea
notes: Much shorter than the European Alps, averaging just over 1000m in height. This is a popular destination for domestic tourism due to the scenery and various landmarks in the region.

title: Mountains of South Korea
notes: The tallest mountain in Gangwon Province (강원도) in the Taebaek Mountains, with a height of 1,708m (5,604ft).

title: Mountains of South Korea
notes: Shield volcano on Jeju Island, which is estimated to have last erupted around 5000 years ago. It is the tallest mountain in South Korea, with a height of 1,947m (6,388ft).

title: Mountains of South Korea
notes: 2nd tallest mountain in South Korea, and the tallest mountain on the mainland, with a height of 1,915m (5,564ft).

title: Seas of South Korea
notes: The Jeju Strait forms the boundary between the Sea of Japan (East Sea) and the Yellow Sea.

title: Seas of South Korea
notes: A Japan-Korea Undersea Tunnel (한일 해저 터널), over 200km long, has been proposed to connect Japan and South Korea along the strait for decades.
